metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` was updated: since the single-eval language at line 112 provably triggers the 'where present' condition, the update is unconditional — add an AC with a `grep` proof (e.g. `grep -n 'test-design.md' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` returns a hit near the Phase 2/Repair section).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md 'is checked for any single-eval RED-artifact wording and, where present, gains a pointer' — framing the update as conditional. Reading the actual file confirms single-sample language IS present at line 112 ('RED = an eval/behavioural assertion that fails against the current instruction' — no mention of multiple samples, threshold, or negative control). The condition is provably true, making the taxonomy update unconditional committed work. Yet no acceptance criterion verifies that llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md was updated.
findings.location: Plan body — rebar-debug skill integration paragraph; no corresponding AC item
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md`'s Phase 2 / Repair section cites the shared eval standard. Prove: `grep -n "test-design.md" examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` returns a hit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` check: either a grep confirming the pointer was added (if the wording was found) or an explicit 'no conflicting wording found' determination documented in the PR; e.g., `grep -n 'test-design' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` or a prose note that the check was performed and no update was needed.
findings.finding: The What section describes a conditional update to `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` ('is checked for any single-eval RED-artifact wording and, where present, gains a pointer'), but no Acceptance Criterion covers this step. The union of ACs cannot verify this work item was completed or skipped with justification.
findings.location: What section – rebar-debug integration bullet: '`llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` is checked for any single-eval RED-artifact wording and, where present, gains a pointer'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies the `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` update: either a grep showing the pointer to `test-design.md` is present where single-eval RED-artifact wording exists, or an explicit 'no change needed' finding if the file contains no such wording (with that conclusion recorded).
findings.finding: The plan body describes a conditional modification to `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` — adding a pointer to the shared eval standard wherever single-eval RED-artifact wording appears — but no acceptance criterion covers this deliverable. It is orphaned described work with no acceptance gate.
findings.location: Plan body (rebar-debug integration paragraph): '`llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` is checked for any single-eval RED-artifact wording and, where present, gains a pointer to the shared standard's quantified-eval requirements so the two files cannot disagree.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '`llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` either (a) contains no single-eval RED-artifact wording, documented as-checked, or (b) includes a pointer to `test-design.md`'s quantified-eval requirements adjacent to any such wording. Prove: `grep -n "test-design" examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` returns a hit, OR a ticket comment records that no single-eval wording was found and no change was needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC item that verifies llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md is either updated (cite the pointer line) or confirmed clean: e.g., `grep -c 'test-design.md\|quantified-eval\|single.*eval' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` shows the pointer added, OR a reviewer note records 'no conflicting wording found — no update needed'.
findings.finding: The plan conditionally updates llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md ('where present, gains a pointer') but no AC item verifies this update was made or that the file is confirmed clean of conflicting single-eval wording — leaving a potential stranded/conflicting reference with no verifiable proof of resolution.
findings.location: What section, rebar-debug skill integration paragraph: 'llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md is checked for any single-eval RED-artifact wording and, where present, gains a pointer...' — no corresponding AC item.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC entry: 'If `grep -n 'single.*eval\|one.*sample\|one RED\|one GREEN' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` hits, verify a pointer to test-design.md appears in that context; if no hits, record "confirmed clean" in a ticket comment. Prove: the grep result (hits+pointer OR no-hits note) is recorded.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` update: specify what to check for (single-eval wording), what change to make if found (pointer to shared standard), and a proof command (e.g., `grep -n 'test-design\|quantified' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md`) so the change is verifiable and cannot be silently skipped.
findings.finding: The `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` update is described as part of the plan's work ('is checked for any single-eval RED-artifact wording and, where present, gains a pointer to the shared standard's quantified-eval requirements so the two files cannot disagree') but has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion or proof command. If this file contains single-eval wording, it could be silently left inconsistent with the new shared standard, undermining the stated intent that 'the two files cannot disagree.'
findings.location: What section — rebar-debug integration paragraph; Acceptance Criteria section (absence)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] If `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` contains single-eval RED-artifact wording, it gains a pointer to `test-design.md`'s quantified-eval requirements. Prove: `grep -n "test-design\|quantified\|multiple samples" examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` returns at least one hit, OR `grep -c "single.*eval\|one.*sample" examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` returns 0 (no uncaveated single-eval wording remains).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Anchor the AC4 grep to the heading pattern: `grep -n '^## Step 4.*Design and prove a RED' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md` to assert the phrase appears specifically in the Step 4 heading line.
findings.finding: AC4's proving command (`grep -n 'Design and prove a RED' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md`) checks that the phrase exists anywhere in the file, but the stated criterion is that it appears 'within the ## Step 4 heading line.' The command cannot distinguish a hit in a heading from a hit in body prose or a different section.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC4: `grep -n 'Design and prove a RED'` … 'hits within the ## Step 4 heading line'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC4 proving command with:\n`grep -n '^## Step 4.*Design and prove a RED' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the AC1 `grep -c` command with a command that verifies EACH required term appears at least once; e.g., run a separate `grep -q` for each term or use `grep -cP` per-term and assert each count >=1.
findings.finding: AC1's `grep -c` with an alternation pattern counts the number of LINES matching any of the 7 terms, not the number of distinct terms present. If 'tier' alone appears on 7+ lines, the command returns >=7 even if 'defect-seeded', 'collateral', etc. are entirely absent from the file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – first AC: `grep -c 'RED oracle\|contract card\|tier\|collateral\|fault\|gating\|defect-seeded' ...` >= 7
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the single `grep -c` with a loop:
```bash
for term in 'RED oracle' 'contract card' 'tier' 'collateral' 'fault' 'gating' 'defect-seeded'; do
  grep -q "$term" examples/claude-skills/shared/test-design.md || echo "MISSING: $term"
done
```
This proves every distinct term is present.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact placement of the quantified-eval pointer within `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` — it must be inserted adjacent to (or replacing) the 'RED artifact is an eval / behavioural assertion' sentence at line 12 AND at the Phase 2 Repair section (lines 112-113) so the constraint is co-located with both points where an executor would consume it.
findings.finding: The plan says llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md 'gains a pointer to the shared standard's quantified-eval requirements' but never specifies WHERE in that file the pointer is placed. The file's eval guidance lives at the end ('Phase 2 (Repair)' section, line 112), while an executor reading top-to-bottom may encounter a preamble pointer and not connect it to Step 4's behavioral context — or vice versa. This is instruction-locality mode #16 as defined by the taxonomy the plan itself governs: the constraint (quantified-eval standard) would be present but not structurally co-located with the step (Phase 2 Step 4 in SKILL.md) where compliance is expected.
findings.location: What section — rebar-debug integration bullet: 'llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md is checked … gains a pointer to the shared standard's quantified-eval requirements'
findings.suggested_fix: In the rebar-debug integration bullet, replace 'gains a pointer' with: 'the single-eval sentence at line 12 and the Phase 2 Repair bullet are each amended in-place to append: "(see test-design.md §LLM-surface evals for the quantified-eval requirements: pinned model/settings, multiple samples, predeclared threshold, negative control)"'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the edit instruction for rebar-debug Step 4's LLM-surface paragraph: specify that the current single-eval sentence is REPLACED (not supplemented) by text that names `test-design.md` as the sole governing standard and summarizes the minimum quantified-eval requirements inline, so no contradictory eval standard remains in the step.
findings.finding: The plan says rebar-debug Step 4's LLM-surface paragraph is 'updated to cite the shared file's quantified-eval requirements as the governing RED-artifact standard, superseding its current single-eval description' — but does not specify whether the current non-quantified paragraph text is removed, replaced, or kept alongside the new citation. The current paragraph (SKILL.md lines 443-446) describes a single-eval RED artifact with no quantification. If only a citation is appended and the original text is retained, two contradictory eval standards will coexist in Step 4: one (the existing paragraph) says a single eval failing for the right reason suffices; the other (the cited test-design.md) mandates multiple samples, a threshold, and a negative control. This is a pink-elephant antipattern risk (taxonomy mode #16/#17): the agent executing Step 4 has two adjacent authoritative-sounding standards and no tie-break rule.
findings.location: What section — rebar-debug integration bullet: 'Step 4's existing LLM-surface paragraph is updated to cite the shared file's quantified-eval requirements as the governing RED-artifact standard, superseding its current single-eval description'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `test_skill_shared_sync.py` auto-discovers skill directories (e.g., by scanning `examples/claude-skills/` for dirs containing a `SKILL.md`) rather than using a hardcoded list, so the drift gate stays complete as new skills are added.
findings.finding: The skill list in `test_skill_shared_sync.py` is hardcoded (rebar-debug, rebar-implement, rebar-brainstorm). Adding a fourth skill requires updating both the copy location and the test's hardcoded list, with no auto-discovery to catch omissions in either direction — a new skill whose directory contains a drifted or missing `test-design.md` will be silently unchecked.
findings.location: Plan body — reference mechanism section; AC item: 'tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py asserts byte-identity of every copy'
findings.suggested_fix: Have the test enumerate skill directories dynamically: `skill_dirs = [d for d in (base / 'examples/claude-skills').iterdir() if d.is_dir() and (d / 'SKILL.md').exists() and d.name != 'shared']`, then assert each has a byte-identical `test-design.md`. This removes the need to update the test when a new skill is added.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete, repo-internal location for the RED evidence (e.g., a committed `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync_red_evidence.txt`, a PR description section, or a `# RED-RUN-EVIDENCE` comment in the test file) so the artifact is verifiable from the repo without a ticket system.
findings.finding: AC3 requires 'recorded RED evidence in a ticket comment,' but the plan names no ticket system and provides no repo-internal artifact path for this evidence. An executor with no issue-tracker access cannot satisfy this sub-criterion, making this portion of AC3 out-of-band and unverifiable from the repo.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3: 'verified RED once by perturbing a copy, then restored… and the recorded RED evidence in a ticket comment'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Record the RED run output as a code comment at the top of `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py` (e.g., `# RED-RUN-EVIDENCE: pytest ... FAILED [1 failed]`) so the evidence is in the repo and reviewable in the PR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md entry (or a brief ADR) capturing the 'real-file copies over symlinks, drift-gated by unit test' decision and its portability rationale, so the architectural intent is discoverable outside the README.
findings.finding: The novel architectural decision — real-file copies (not symlinks) as the canonical sharing mechanism, drift-gated by a unit test — is documented in README.md but the plan does not mention updating CLAUDE.md or an ADR, leaving future contributors who look for design rationale in the project's architectural docs without a pointer.
findings.location: AC item: 'examples/claude-skills/README.md documents the convention'; plan body: 'Symlinks were considered and rejected'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate rebar-janitor as in-scope or out-of-scope for the test-design.md copy convention, and update the sync one-liner, drift test skill list, and README accordingly to reflect that decision.
findings.finding: rebar-janitor is an unenumerated consumer: it exists at `examples/claude-skills/rebar-janitor/` with a Phase 5 ticketization step that authors tickets with acceptance criteria — the same trigger condition that drives the rebar-brainstorm integration — yet the plan neither includes a test-design.md copy for rebar-janitor nor explicitly acknowledges it as out-of-scope.
findings.location: What / Reference mechanism section; AC for byte-identical copies; README sync one-liner

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `test_skill_shared_sync.py` discovers skill directories dynamically (e.g., scan `examples/claude-skills/` for subdirectories containing a `SKILL.md`, excluding `shared/`) rather than using a hardcoded list, so the 'FAILS when a copy diverges or is missing' guarantee holds for any future skill without requiring a test update.
findings.finding: The plan's drift-gate test (`test_skill_shared_sync.py`) implicitly operates on a hardcoded skill list (the sync one-liner names `rebar-debug rebar-implement rebar-brainstorm` statically), yet the plan's AC claims it 'FAILS when a copy diverges or is missing' — a guarantee that is only true for those three skills. The repo already has a fourth skill directory (`examples/claude-skills/rebar-janitor/`) with a `SKILL.md`. If the test uses a hardcoded list, adding a skill in the future without a `test-design.md` copy produces no gate failure, silently undermining the 'every copy' invariant the plan asserts. The existing repo drift-gate convention (prompt-index, env-vars, public-types) auto-derives the full corpus from the filesystem on each run rather than maintaining a static list.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Reference mechanism' section (sync one-liner); AC item 3: 'tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py asserts byte-identity of every copy against the canonical file, FAILS when a copy diverges or is missing'
findings.suggested_fix: In the test, derive the skill set from the filesystem: `skill_dirs = [d for d in (ROOT / 'examples/claude-skills').iterdir() if d.is_dir() and d.name != 'shared' and (d / 'SKILL.md').exists()]`, then assert each has a byte-identical `test-design.md`. Update the sync one-liner to match: `for s in $(ls examples/claude-skills/ | grep -v shared); do cp ...; done` or enumerate via `find`. This aligns with the repo's existing drift-gate convention.

findings.finding: AC3 bundles three distinct verifiable sub-claims for rebar-debug/SKILL.md (Step 4 retitled, LLM-surface paragraph updated to defer to shared standard, contract card added as fourth handoff item) but provides no grep/diff proving command for any of them — an executor could complete two of three changes with no failing check to catch the omission.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3
findings.suggested_fix:
Append to AC3:
```
Verify with:
  grep -q 'Design and prove a RED oracle' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md
  grep -q 'contract card' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md
  grep -q 'shared/test-design.md' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md  # already in AC2; kept for locality
```
(The exact strings can be adjusted to match the authored text, but the commands should be stated.)

findings.finding: AC4 (README documents the shared/ convention) has no concrete proving command — the completion verdict relies entirely on a human reading the file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: `Verify with: grep -q 'shared/' examples/claude-skills/README.md`

findings.finding: AC2's verification command (`grep -l 'shared/test-design.md' ...`) can only confirm the string exists somewhere in each SKILL.md, but the AC text additionally claims 'each reference sitting at the integration point named above' — a placement guarantee the grep cannot check. The AC text makes a stronger claim than its verify step can satisfy.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (grep line)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace or supplement the `grep -l` command with a check that also validates context: e.g., for rebar-debug verify that the reference appears within a few lines of 'Step 4' or 'Phase 2'; for rebar-implement within 'Phase 4a' or '4b'; for rebar-brainstorm within 'Before recording tickets'. A minimal approach: document in the AC that a human reviewer must confirm placement at the named section during review, making the verification requirement explicit even if not fully automatable.

findings.finding: AC #3 verifies rebar-debug's Step 4 retitling, LLM-eval paragraph update, and Step 5 contract-card handoff, but does NOT include an observable proof that Step 5's mutation check is updated to be defect-seeded — a named body goal that has no corresponding acceptance criterion check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #3; Plan body section 'Skill integration — rebar-debug'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC #3: 'Step 5's mutation-check instruction names defect-seeded mutation (re-introduce the exact defect from the confirmed root cause), replacing any arbitrary-negation wording.'

findings.finding: The plan introduces LLM-surface eval requirements in test-design.md (pinned model/settings, paired before/after prompts, multiple samples with predeclared threshold, negative control, quantified failure rates) that conflict with the existing LLM-surface RED-artifact protocol in rebar-debug's Step 4 and llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 (which only requires 'an eval or behavioural assertion that fails against the current instruction'). The plan never states whether test-design.md supersedes, augments, or is reconciled with the existing llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 spec — leaving two co-resident specifications with divergent requirements for what constitutes a valid LLM-surface RED artifact.
findings.location: Plan section: 'LLM-surface evals' bullet; rebar-debug SKILL.md Step 4 (line 444–446); examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 (lines 112–114)
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's rebar-debug integration section, add: 'Step 4's LLM-surface paragraph is updated to cite test-design.md's LLM-surface eval requirements (pinned model, multiple samples, predeclared threshold, negative control) as the governing RED-artifact standard, superseding the single-eval description currently in that paragraph and in llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2.'

findings.finding: The rebar-implement integration description says only 'Phase 4a/4b reference the shared file for oracle design (inventory, tier, oracle completeness, gating proof); held-out structure unchanged' without specifying the form of the reference (a load instruction like the rebar-debug Phase 2 entry point, a new bullet, an inline sentence, a subsection). An executor must invent the structural form, which may produce an inconsistent integration compared to rebar-debug's precisely described changes.
findings.location: Skill integration — rebar-implement paragraph

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `../shared/test-design.md` 'resolves both in this repo and in a skills installation where `shared/` sits beside the skill directories,' but this cross-directory relative-path loading is an unverified capability claim. The only existing precedent for a supplementary reference file in a skill (`llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` in rebar-debug) is referenced as 'in this skill's directory' — a same-directory path — not a `../` cross-directory path. The plan does not confirm that Claude Code's skill execution environment follows `../` relative paths from prose instructions in SKILL.md, nor specify the exact loading mechanism (an explicit tool call instruction vs. bare prose prose naming the path).
findings.location: Plan 'What' section, paragraph 2: 'each SKILL.md naming the path relative to its own directory (`../shared/test-design.md`) plus its repo location, so the reference resolves both in this repo and in a skills installation where `shared/` sits beside the skill directories'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
